title: Grant ESO write access on Keystone KV paths
description:
# GitHub Issue #249: Grant ESO write capabilities on Keystone KV paths so fernet/credential PushSecrets succeed

**Category**: bug | **Scope**: Small

**Description**: The Keystone operator creates `PushSecret` CRs to back up the `*-fernet-keys` and `*-credential-keys` Secrets to OpenBao under `kv-v2/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `.../credential-keys` (`operators/keystone/internal/controller/reconcile_fernet.go:332-358`, `operators/keystone/internal/controller/reconcile_credential.go:323-349`). Both `PushSecret` CRs reference `ClusterSecretStore openbao-cluster-store` (`deploy/eso/clustersecretstore.yaml:17`), which authenticates via the `eso-management` Kubernetes auth role (`deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh:85-91`, `token_policies=eso-management`). The bound policy `deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl:27-29` grants ONLY `read` on `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/*`, so every push fails with a 403 and the KV paths for fernet/credential keys remain empty. The only Keystone-related material actually present in OpenBao is what the bootstrap script writes directly with the root token (`deploy/openbao/bootstrap/write-bootstrap-secrets.sh:84-92`): `bootstrap/keystone-admin`, `infrastructure/mariadb`, `openstack/keystone/db`. Introduce a dedicated push-scope policy (mirroring `deploy/openbao/policies/push-app-credentials.hcl` and `push-ceph-keys.hcl`, which use `capabilities = [\"create\", \"update\", \"read\"]`) and bind it additionally to the `eso-management` role so the read-vs-write separation of concerns is preserved.

**Motivation**: The `PushSecret` backup is the only mechanism that makes rotated fernet and credential keys recoverable from OpenBao. Without it, key material lives exclusively in the in-cluster Kubernetes Secret — a cluster rebuild, operator replay, or accidental Secret deletion permanently loses every fernet/credential key ever generated or rotated, invalidating all issued Fernet tokens and rendering every existing application credential in the DB undecryptable. The operator already builds the correct `PushSecret` CRs and the target KV paths are the right ones; the gap is purely on the OpenBao authorization side. Keeping the read-only `eso-management` policy untouched and adding a separate `push-keystone-keys` policy mirrors the pattern already established for Ceph keys and application credentials, and keeps least-privilege intact (the push role cannot read unrelated paths, the management role cannot write).

**Affected Areas**:
- `deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` (new file — `create`, `update`, `read` on `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `.../credential-keys`)
- `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh` (bind additional policy to the `eso-management` role: `token_policies=eso-management,push-keystone-keys`)
- `architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md` (document the new policy alongside the existing `eso-management` / `push-*` policies)

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] New policy file `deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` exists with two path blocks (`kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys`, `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/credential-keys`) each granting `capabilities = [\"create\", \"update\", \"read\"]`; no wildcard path, no `delete`, no `list`
- [ ] `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh` binds `token_policies=eso-management,push-keystone-keys` on the management cluster's `eso-management` role; other cluster roles (`control-plane`, `hypervisor`, `storage`) are unchanged
- [ ] `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-policies.sh` picks up the new `.hcl` automatically (no change needed; verified via `ls deploy/openbao/policies/*.hcl`)
- [ ] `deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl` stays read-only — the existing DEVIATION comment (lines 19-26) is updated to point at the new push policy for write access
- [ ] On a fresh kind cluster, after `make deploy-infra` plus a Keystone CR reconcile, `bao kv get kv-v2/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `.../credential-keys` both return the current key set (not \"no value found\")
- [ ] `kubectl -n openstack describe pushsecret <keystone>-fernet-keys-backup` reports `SecretSynced=True`; the same for `<keystone>-credential-keys-backup`
- [ ] New Chainsaw e2e step (extending `tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/` or a dedicated `openbao-pushsecret-sync` test) asserts both `PushSecret` CRs reach `Ready=True` within the existing timeout budget

**Non-Goals**:
- Changing the `PushSecret` target paths or the `ClusterSecretStore` wiring. The push targets (`kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/{fernet,credential}-keys`) are correct and already match the naming used by the operator.
- Widening the `eso-management` policy to include write verbs. The read/push separation is intentional and matches the pattern of `push-app-credentials` vs `eso-*` policies.
- Cleaning up orphaned OpenBao paths after CR deletion — that is already tracked by #193 (finalizer for OpenBao PushSecret cleanup).
- Re-scoping what the rotation CronJob is allowed to write — covered by #190 (restrict rotation CronJob SA to read-only).

**Labels:** bug

**Labels:** bug

---
Source: https://github.com/C5C3/forge/issues/249

stories.title: Platform operator gains durable recovery of Keystone fernet and credential keys in OpenBao
stories.want: the Keystone operator's `PushSecret` CRs for fernet-keys and credential-keys to actually succeed against OpenBao
stories.so_that: rotated key material is durably backed up and recoverable after a cluster rebuild, operator replay, or accidental Secret deletion — preventing loss of every issued Fernet token and every encrypted application credential (CC-0083)
criteria: After `make deploy-infra` plus a Keystone CR reconcile on a fresh kind cluster, `bao kv get kv-v2/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` returns the current fernet key set (not `no value found`)
After the same run, `bao kv get kv-v2/openstack/keystone/credential-keys` returns the current credential key set
`kubectl -n openstack describe pushsecret <keystone>-fernet-keys-backup` reports `SecretSynced=True`
`kubectl -n openstack describe pushsecret <keystone>-credential-keys-backup` reports `SecretSynced=True`
No 403 `permission denied` errors appear in external-secrets controller logs for the two backup PushSecret CRs

stories.title: Security reviewer can verify least-privilege separation between read and write policies on Keystone KV paths
stories.want: write capabilities on Keystone fernet/credential KV paths expressed in a dedicated `push-keystone-keys` policy, not merged into `eso-management`
stories.so_that: the read-only `eso-management` policy stays read-only, and the push policy is narrow (two exact paths, no wildcard, no delete/list) — mirroring the pattern already used for `push-ceph-keys` and `push-app-credentials` (CC-0083)
criteria: `deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` exists and contains exactly two `path` blocks: `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/credential-keys`
Each path block grants exactly `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]` — no `delete`, no `list`, no other verbs
No wildcard path (`*`) appears anywhere in `push-keystone-keys.hcl`
`deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl` is unchanged except for the DEVIATION comment, and still grants only `read` capabilities on all of its paths
The DEVIATION comment in `eso-management.hcl` points readers at `push-keystone-keys.hcl` for write access, so the separation is self-documenting

stories.title: Bootstrap scripts pick up the new policy and bind it only to the management cluster's ESO role
stories.want: `setup-policies.sh` to apply `push-keystone-keys.hcl` automatically and `setup-auth.sh` to attach it only to `eso-management`
stories.so_that: running `make deploy-infra` (or re-running the bootstrap) produces a correctly-authorised management ESO role without extra manual steps, while control-plane / hypervisor / storage roles remain unchanged (CC-0083)
criteria: `setup-policies.sh` writes the `push-keystone-keys` policy without code changes (it already globs `*.hcl` — verified by `ls deploy/openbao/policies/*.hcl` listing the new file)
Running `setup-auth.sh` writes `token_policies=eso-management,push-keystone-keys` on `auth/kubernetes/management/role/eso-management`
Running `setup-auth.sh` still writes `token_policies=eso-control-plane` on `auth/kubernetes/control-plane/role/eso-control-plane` (unchanged)
Running `setup-auth.sh` still writes `token_policies=eso-hypervisor` on `auth/kubernetes/hypervisor/role/eso-hypervisor` (unchanged)
Running `setup-auth.sh` still writes `token_policies=eso-storage` on `auth/kubernetes/storage/role/eso-storage` (unchanged)
Re-running `setup-auth.sh` after it has already succeeded is idempotent (the role write is an upsert — no duplicate/extra policies appear)

stories.title: Documentation reader finds the new policy described alongside the existing push and eso policies
stories.want: `architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md` to document `push-keystone-keys.hcl` in the PushSecret Policies section and include it in the Apply Policies loop
stories.so_that: an engineer reading the OpenBao deployment doc gets an accurate, complete picture of authorisation for Keystone key backups — the doc no longer diverges from the deployed policy set (CC-0083)
criteria: The PushSecret Policies subsection of `09-openbao-deployment.md` shows a code block for `push-keystone-keys.hcl` with both exact paths and `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]`
The Apply Policies loop (currently at `09-openbao-deployment.md:360-362`) includes `push-keystone-keys` in the `for` list
The ESO Management Policy subsection (or a nearby callout) cross-references `push-keystone-keys` as the source of write access for Keystone KV paths
The doc's capability verbs for `push-keystone-keys` match the actual HCL file (`create`, `update`, `read`) — no drift

stories.title: CI asserts PushSecret sync for Keystone backups end-to-end
stories.want: the Chainsaw test suite to assert that both `keystone-*-fernet-keys-backup` and `keystone-*-credential-keys-backup` PushSecret CRs reach a synced state after a Keystone deployment
stories.so_that: a regression that re-introduces the 403 (e.g. a future policy refactor that drops `push-keystone-keys`, or a role binding that forgets to include it) is caught automatically in CI rather than discovered later on a rebuild (CC-0083)
criteria: `tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/chainsaw-test.yaml` includes an assertion that the PushSecret `keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup` has status condition `Ready=True` (or the equivalent ESO v1alpha1 `SecretSynced=True`)
The same test includes an assertion for `keystone-basic-credential-keys-backup`
Assertions complete within the existing `timeouts.assert: 5m` budget — no new or increased timeout is required
The catch block captures PushSecret status and external-secrets controller logs on failure so a future 403 is diagnosable from CI output alone
The existing basic-deployment happy-path assertions continue to pass (no regression)

requirements.description: The system SHALL define a `push-keystone-keys` OpenBao policy that grants exactly `create`, `update`, `read` capabilities on the two explicit KV paths `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/credential-keys`, with no wildcard and no other verbs (CC-0083).
requirements.rationale: The PushSecret backup path must succeed, but least-privilege requires that the write scope is limited to exactly the two fernet/credential paths — not the whole `openstack/keystone/*` subtree (which contains read-only `db` secrets) and not the whole `openstack/*` subtree. Mirrors the pattern used by `push-ceph-keys.hcl` and `push-app-credentials.hcl`.

scenarios.name: Policy file contains both exact paths
scenarios.then: it contains exactly two `path` stanzas with literal keys `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/credential-keys`

scenarios.name: Each path grants exactly create/update/read
scenarios.when: each path stanza's `capabilities` list is inspected
scenarios.then: it contains exactly the three strings `create`, `update`, `read`

scenarios.name: Policy is loaded cleanly by OpenBao
scenarios.when: `bao policy write push-keystone-keys deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` is executed against a running OpenBao
scenarios.then: the command exits 0 and `bao policy read push-keystone-keys` returns the same content
and_then: no HCL parse errors are reported

requirements.description: The bootstrap script `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh` SHALL bind the `push-keystone-keys` policy to the management cluster's `eso-management` Kubernetes auth role in addition to `eso-management`, and SHALL NOT modify the policy bindings of the `eso-control-plane`, `eso-hypervisor`, or `eso-storage` roles (CC-0083).
requirements.rationale: Only the management cluster's ESO instance authenticates the Keystone fernet/credential PushSecrets against OpenBao (via `ClusterSecretStore openbao-cluster-store`). The other cluster roles must stay unchanged to keep least-privilege and match the behaviour documented for CC-0009.

scenarios.name: Management role gets both policies
and_then: no other policies are added by this change

scenarios.name: Other cluster roles are unchanged
scenarios.then: the `eso-control-plane`, `eso-hypervisor`, and `eso-storage` roles each still have a single policy binding (`eso-<cluster>`)

scenarios.name: Script is idempotent
scenarios.when: `setup-auth.sh` is re-run on an already-bootstrapped OpenBao
and_then: the management role's policy list is still exactly `eso-management,push-keystone-keys` (no duplicates, no drift)

requirements.description: The `deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl` policy SHALL remain read-only (only `read` capabilities on all paths), and its DEVIATION comment SHALL be updated to direct readers to `push-keystone-keys.hcl` for write access on the Keystone fernet/credential paths (CC-0083).
requirements.rationale: Keeping `eso-management` read-only preserves the audit property that a leaked management ESO token cannot write to OpenBao. The existing DEVIATION comment (lines 19-26) explains why `openstack/keystone/*` appears in a management policy at all; it must now also explain that write access lives in a separate policy so a future reader does not merge the two.

scenarios.name: DEVIATION comment references push policy
scenarios.when: the comment block starting with `# DEVIATION` in `eso-management.hcl` is read
scenarios.then: it mentions `push-keystone-keys.hcl` (or the `push-keystone-keys` policy) as the source of write access for `openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `openstack/keystone/credential-keys`
and_then: the existing rationale about the keystone-db ExternalSecret is preserved
the feature reference stays `CC-0009` for the original deviation and adds a `CC-0083` reference for the push split

requirements.description: The architecture document `architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md` SHALL document the new `push-keystone-keys.hcl` policy in the PushSecret Policies section and SHALL include `push-keystone-keys` in the Apply Policies loop (CC-0083).
requirements.rationale: The architecture doc currently lists every policy that ships with OpenBao. Omitting `push-keystone-keys` would create a drift between documented and deployed policies — the exact failure mode that motivated this bug (the `eso-management` policy doc matched the HCL, but the operator needed more).

scenarios.name: PushSecret Policies section shows the new policy
scenarios.when: the PushSecret Policies subsection (around lines 308-324) is read
scenarios.then: it contains an HCL code block for `push-keystone-keys.hcl` with both path literals and `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]`
and_then: the capability list in the doc matches the capability list in the HCL file exactly

scenarios.name: Apply Policies loop references the new policy
scenarios.when: the Apply Policies code block (around lines 358-363) is read
scenarios.then: the policy name `push-keystone-keys` appears in the `for policy in ...` list
and_then: the list order keeps the grouping (push-* policies next to each other)

scenarios.name: ESO Management Policy cross-reference
scenarios.when: the ESO Management Policy subsection is read
scenarios.then: it includes a note (sentence or callout) that write access for Keystone fernet/credential paths is granted via `push-keystone-keys`, not by extending `eso-management`
and_then: the reader can trace the ESO `openstack/keystone/*` read access to the HCL and the write access to the new push policy

requirements.description: The Chainsaw e2e suite SHALL assert that both Keystone backup PushSecret CRs reach a synced state within the existing timeout budget after a Keystone deployment (CC-0083).
requirements.rationale: This bug was not caught by the existing basic-deployment test because that test only asserted the PushSecret CR's existence, not its sync status. An assertion on sync status (`Ready=True` / `SecretSynced=True`) closes the regression window.

scenarios.name: Fernet backup PushSecret reaches synced state
scenarios.when: `chainsaw test tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment` runs on a cluster with the new policy applied
scenarios.then: the test asserts `keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup` reports `Ready=True` (or the equivalent ESO v1alpha1 `SecretSynced=True`) status condition

scenarios.name: Credential backup PushSecret reaches synced state
Regression path: missing policy is detected
scenarios.when: `push-keystone-keys.hcl` is removed and the bootstrap re-runs
scenarios.then: the Chainsaw assertion times out and the `catch` block emits PushSecret status and external-secrets logs showing a 403
and_then: a future regression is diagnosable from CI output alone

requirements.description: The `push-keystone-keys` policy MUST NOT grant write access to `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/db` or any other path outside the two fernet/credential literals (CC-0083).
requirements.rationale: `openstack/keystone/db` holds the MariaDB credentials that Keystone reads, not writes. Granting write access there would let a compromised ESO controller overwrite DB credentials and lock Keystone out of its own database. This requirement is a security floor that guards against lazy refactors (e.g. replacing the two literals with `openstack/keystone/*`).

scenarios.name: db path is not in the policy
scenarios.when: the HCL file is grep'd for `keystone/db`

scenarios.name: Wildcard on keystone subtree is not in the policy
scenarios.when: the HCL file is grep'd for `keystone/*` or `keystone/\*`

scenarios.name: A token with only push-keystone-keys cannot write to db
scenarios.when: a test token bound only to `push-keystone-keys` attempts `bao kv put kv-v2/openstack/keystone/db foo=bar`
and_then: this is documented as the intended behaviour in the policy comment

tasks.title: Create `deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` with exactly two `path` stanzas (`kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys`, `kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/credential-keys`), each with `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]`, SPDX header, and a top comment citing CC-0083 and referencing `push-app-credentials.hcl` as the pattern source. No wildcard paths. (REQ-001, REQ-006)

Update the DEVIATION comment block in `deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl` (lines 19-26) to state that write access for `openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `openstack/keystone/credential-keys` is granted separately by `push-keystone-keys.hcl`; add a `CC-0083` feature reference alongside the existing `CC-0009`. Do NOT add or change any `capabilities` list in this file. (REQ-003)

In `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh`, split the `for cluster in ...` ESO-role loop so the management cluster writes `token_policies=eso-management,push-keystone-keys` while the other three clusters keep `token_policies=eso-${cluster}`. Preserve idempotence (role write is already an upsert). Add a brief inline comment citing `CC-0083` that explains why the management role alone gets the extra policy. Run `shellcheck` to verify the script is clean. (REQ-002)

Verify no change is needed to `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-policies.sh`. Confirm by (a) running `ls deploy/openbao/policies/*.hcl` locally and visually checking `push-keystone-keys.hcl` is picked up by the existing `*.hcl` glob, and (b) adding a one-line comment in the PR description citing the verification — no code change. (REQ-001, REQ-002)

Update `architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md`: in the PushSecret Policies subsection (around lines 308-324) add an HCL code block for `push-keystone-keys.hcl` showing both exact paths and `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]`. In the ESO Management Policy subsection add a callout sentence noting that write access for Keystone fernet/credential paths lives in `push-keystone-keys`, not `eso-management`. Ensure the documented capability verbs match the actual HCL exactly. (REQ-004)

Update the Apply Policies shell loop in `architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md` (around lines 358-363) to include `push-keystone-keys` in the `for policy in ...` list, placed next to `push-ceph-keys` and `push-app-credentials` to keep push-* policies grouped. (REQ-004)

Extend `tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/chainsaw-test.yaml` Step 4 (Fernet resources) to assert `status.conditions[?type == 'Ready'].status == 'True'` on `PushSecret keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup`. First inspect the installed `external-secrets.io/v1alpha1` PushSecret CRD to confirm the exact condition type name (`Ready` vs `SecretSynced`) by running `kubectl explain pushsecret.status.conditions.type` against a running cluster and choose the matching JMESPath. Keep the existing existence assertion; add the status assertion immediately after it. Do not change `timeouts.assert`. (REQ-005)

Add an analogous assertion for `PushSecret keystone-basic-credential-keys-backup` in `tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/chainsaw-test.yaml` — same condition path and timeout, placed in a Step 4-analogue section for credential resources (create one if none exists). (REQ-005)

Extend the `catch` block of the new PushSecret assertion step(s) in `tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/chainsaw-test.yaml` to run `kubectl -n openstack describe pushsecret keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup keystone-basic-credential-keys-backup` and `kubectl -n external-secrets logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-secrets --tail=200`, so a future 403 regression is diagnosable from CI output alone. Reuse the existing catch script pattern (shell heredoc with `echo '=== ... ==='` headers). (REQ-005)

Perform one-time regression validation (NOT committed to CI): locally remove `push-keystone-keys.hcl`, re-run bootstrap, re-run the Chainsaw basic-deployment test, and confirm the new assertions fail with a catch block showing 403 from external-secrets. Record the evidence (log excerpt) in the PR description. Restore the file. (REQ-005)

Final self-check before review: run the whole new Chainsaw assertion suite on a fresh kind cluster via `make deploy-infra && chainsaw test tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment`. Confirm both PushSecret CRs reach the synced condition within the existing 5m budget and that `bao kv get kv-v2/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `.../credential-keys` return non-empty data (this is user-facing acceptance). (REQ-005, REQ-001, REQ-002)

test_specifications.story: Security reviewer can verify least-privilege separation between read and write policies on Keystone KV paths
test_specifications.expected: File exists with exactly two `path` stanzas (fernet-keys, credential-keys), each with `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]`; no wildcard, no delete, no list. Verified by existing policy-loading script plus a grep-based assertion in the Chainsaw test's catch block or a shell assertion in the policy review checklist.

test_specifications.story: Security reviewer can verify least-privilege separation between read and write policies on Keystone KV paths
test_specifications.expected: `grep -E 'keystone/\*|keystone/db' deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` returns no matches. Enforced as a review-checklist line item (REQ-006).

test_specifications.story: Bootstrap scripts pick up the new policy and bind it only to the management cluster's ESO role
test_specifications.expected: `shellcheck deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh` passes; a review-time grep confirms that `token_policies=eso-management,push-keystone-keys` is written for the management cluster and that the control-plane/hypervisor/storage loop iteration writes only `token_policies=eso-<cluster>`.

test_specifications.story: Bootstrap scripts pick up the new policy and bind it only to the management cluster's ESO role
test_specifications.expected: A Chainsaw `script` step runs `bao read -format=json auth/kubernetes/management/role/eso-management` (via the bootstrap pod) and asserts the JSON `.data.token_policies` array contains both `eso-management` and `push-keystone-keys`. A follow-up script reads the three other roles and asserts each contains only its single `eso-<cluster>` policy.

test_specifications.story: Security reviewer can verify least-privilege separation between read and write policies on Keystone KV paths
test_specifications.expected: Every `capabilities = [...]` assignment in the file is exactly `["read"]`. Enforced by `grep -nE 'capabilities\s*=' deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl | grep -vE 'capabilities\s*=\s*\["read"\]'` returning no matches.

test_specifications.story: Security reviewer can verify least-privilege separation between read and write policies on Keystone KV paths
test_specifications.expected: The DEVIATION block mentions `push-keystone-keys` explicitly so a reader following the comment reaches the policy that grants write access. Enforced by `grep -q 'push-keystone-keys' deploy/openbao/policies/eso-management.hcl`.

test_specifications.story: Documentation reader finds the new policy described alongside the existing push and eso policies
test_specifications.expected: `grep -A3 'push-keystone-keys.hcl' architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md` shows both path literals (fernet-keys, credential-keys) and `capabilities = ["create", "update", "read"]` — matching the HCL file exactly.

test_specifications.story: Documentation reader finds the new policy described alongside the existing push and eso policies
test_specifications.expected: The Apply Policies `for` loop contains the literal `push-keystone-keys`. Enforced by `grep -E 'for policy in' -A2 architecture/docs/09-implementation/09-openbao-deployment.md | grep -q push-keystone-keys`.

test_specifications.story: CI asserts PushSecret sync for Keystone backups end-to-end
test_specifications.expected: An `assert` step on `PushSecret keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup` checks `status.conditions[?type == 'Ready'].status == 'True'` (or the ESO v1alpha1 `SecretSynced=True` equivalent present on the installed CRD) and passes within `timeouts.assert: 5m`.

test_specifications.story: CI asserts PushSecret sync for Keystone backups end-to-end
test_specifications.expected: An `assert` step on `PushSecret keystone-basic-credential-keys-backup` checks the same `Ready=True` / `SecretSynced=True` condition within the existing timeout budget.

test_specifications.story: CI asserts PushSecret sync for Keystone backups end-to-end
test_specifications.expected: The new step's `catch` block runs `kubectl -n openstack describe pushsecret keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup keystone-basic-credential-keys-backup` and `kubectl -n external-secrets logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=external-secrets --tail=200` so a future 403 regression is diagnosable from CI output alone.

test_specifications.story: Platform operator gains durable recovery of Keystone fernet and credential keys in OpenBao
test_specifications.expected: Manual one-time validation: with `push-keystone-keys.hcl` removed before bootstrap, the new assert step fails and the catch block emits a 403 from external-secrets. This confirms the test actually detects the bug class it is meant to catch. Documented in the PR description; not re-run in CI.

test_specifications.story: Platform operator gains durable recovery of Keystone fernet and credential keys in OpenBao
test_specifications.expected: A `script` step execs into the openbao-0 pod and runs `bao kv get kv-v2/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys` and `.../credential-keys`, asserting both return non-empty data (grep `key-` in the output). Guards against the operator writing a Secret with no keys or ESO failing silently.

review_criteria: BLOCKER: `push-keystone-keys.hcl` contains exactly two literal `path` stanzas — no wildcard, no `keystone/db`, no `keystone/*`. Verified by inspection and by `grep -E 'keystone/\*|keystone/db' deploy/openbao/policies/push-keystone-keys.hcl` returning no matches (REQ-001, REQ-006).
BLOCKER: Every `capabilities = [...]` in `push-keystone-keys.hcl` is exactly `["create", "update", "read"]`; no `delete`, no `list`, no other verbs (REQ-001).

BLOCKER: `setup-auth.sh` binds `token_policies=eso-management,push-keystone-keys` only on the management cluster role; `eso-control-plane`, `eso-hypervisor`, `eso-storage` role policy bindings are byte-for-byte unchanged (REQ-002).
BLOCKER: `eso-management.hcl` `capabilities` lists are all `["read"]` — unchanged from pre-PR (REQ-003).

BLOCKER: Chainsaw basic-deployment test passes on a fresh kind cluster with the new policy, and the new assertions fail (with 403 visible in catch output) when the policy is removed — this second half is documented in the PR description as manual validation (REQ-005).
CRITICAL: `eso-management.hcl` DEVIATION comment references `push-keystone-keys` explicitly so the separation is self-documenting and a future reader will not merge the two policies (REQ-003).
CRITICAL: Architecture doc and HCL file are in sync — capability verbs, path literals, and Apply Policies loop all match. Documented via `grep` check comparing doc and HCL (REQ-004).

CRITICAL: All files added or changed carry an SPDX header matching the project convention (`SPDX-FileCopyrightText: Copyright 2026 SAP SE or an SAP affiliate company`, `SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0`) and a `CC-0083` feature reference in the top comment or inline rationale (CC-0083, Rule 11).
CRITICAL: `shellcheck deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh` exits 0; no new warnings introduced.
MINOR: Chainsaw catch block captures `describe pushsecret` and external-secrets controller logs so a future 403 is diagnosable from CI output without re-running the job (REQ-005).
MINOR: In `setup-auth.sh`, the loop refactor keeps the existing `log` messages' tone and idempotence narrative; no unrelated cleanup is bundled into the PR (Rule 4/6).
MINOR: Commit message and PR body cite the feature ID `CC-0083` and link to issue #249 (Rule 11).
implementation_notes:
## Architecture (CC-0083)

### What the deployed state looks like today vs after this change

```
  Today (broken)                              After CC-0083 (fixed)
  ──────────────                              ─────────────────────

  ClusterSecretStore openbao-cluster-store   ClusterSecretStore (unchanged)
         │                                           │
         │ (k8s auth, SA external-secrets/eso)       │
         ▼                                           ▼
  auth/kubernetes/management/role/eso-mgmt    auth/kubernetes/management/role/eso-mgmt
    token_policies=eso-management                token_policies=eso-management,
                                                               push-keystone-keys
         │                                           │
         ▼                                           ▼
  eso-management.hcl                          eso-management.hcl (unchanged)
    read: bootstrap/*, infrastructure/*,        read: bootstrap/*, infrastructure/*,
          openstack/keystone/*                         openstack/keystone/*
                                              push-keystone-keys.hcl  (new)
                                                create/update/read:
                                                  openstack/keystone/fernet-keys
                                                  openstack/keystone/credential-keys

  PushSecret fernet-keys-backup → 403         PushSecret fernet-keys-backup → 200
  PushSecret credential-keys-backup → 403     PushSecret credential-keys-backup → 200
```

### Data flow on a fernet rotate (after fix)

```
  CronJob fernet-rotate
      │ writes
      ▼
  Secret keystone-basic-fernet-keys (k8s)
      │ watched by
      ▼
  PushSecret keystone-basic-fernet-keys-backup
      │ uses ClusterSecretStore openbao-cluster-store
      ▼
  ESO controller → OpenBao k8s auth (management mount)
      │ token has policies: eso-management + push-keystone-keys
      ▼
  KV write to kv-v2/data/openstack/keystone/fernet-keys  (authorised by push-keystone-keys)
```

### Design decisions

1. **Separate policy, not extended `eso-management`**. Preserves the audit invariant that a leaked management-cluster ESO token cannot *write* to OpenBao on its own — the write capability is carried by a second, narrowly-scoped policy attached to the same role. This mirrors the pattern already in place for `push-ceph-keys` (vs `eso-storage`) and `push-app-credentials`.
2. **Exact-literal paths, not `openstack/keystone/*`**. The `openstack/keystone/*` subtree also contains `db` (MariaDB credentials that Keystone only reads). Granting write on the whole subtree would let a compromised ESO controller rewrite DB credentials. Two literal paths is ugly-but-correct; see REQ-006.
3. **Only the management cluster role gets the extra policy**. The PushSecret CRs live in the `openstack` namespace on the management cluster and are resolved by management-cluster ESO via `ClusterSecretStore openbao-cluster-store`. Control-plane / hypervisor / storage ESO instances do not reconcile these PushSecrets.
4. **Policy name singular-"keys" not -"keys-backup"**. Chose `push-keystone-keys` (mirrors `push-ceph-keys`) over `push-keystone-keys-backup` to match the existing naming pattern. The doc and the HCL must agree.
5. **No `delete`, no `list`**. PushSecret never needs to delete (deletion is covered by the separate finalizer work in CC-0079) and never needs to list (it knows the exact remote key). Adding those verbs would violate least-privilege without enabling any feature.

### Key files to follow

- Pattern source for HCL: `deploy/openbao/policies/push-app-credentials.hcl`, `deploy/openbao/policies/push-ceph-keys.hcl` — both are 12-line files with a single `path` stanza and a feature comment.
- Pattern source for setup-auth loop split: `deploy/openbao/bootstrap/setup-auth.sh:83-92` — currently a uniform loop; will become a loop + one conditional branch or a pre-loop lookup table mapping cluster → extra-policies.
- Pattern source for Chainsaw PushSecret assertion: `tests/e2e/keystone/basic-deployment/chainsaw-test.yaml:161-167` — already asserts existence; needs a `status.conditions[?type == ...]` assertion added in the same block. Confirm the exact condition type (`Ready` vs `SecretSynced`) against the installed `external-secrets.io/v1alpha1` CRD before committing the JMESPath.

### Pitfalls

- **CRD version drift**. The project uses `external-secrets.io/v1alpha1` (per the existing basic-deployment test). If an ESO upgrade switches to `v1beta1` or the condition type is renamed in a later release, the assertion's JMESPath will silently stop matching. Mitigation: explicitly name the condition in a comment and verify the JMESPath against a running cluster before committing (see Task 4.1 step).
- **Policy loop order**. `setup-policies.sh` applies policies in glob order. Since `push-keystone-keys` sorts before other `push-*` files alphabetically, this is fine, but the bootstrap doc's Apply Policies loop should keep the explicit order readable (push-* grouped together).
- **`bao` CLI output format**. The verification step uses `bao read -format=json ...` — the JSON field name is `token_policies` (plural). Do not use the human-readable text output for assertions (its whitespace varies by CLI version).
- **Idempotence regression**. The existing `setup-auth.sh` role write is inherently idempotent (upsert). The loop split must preserve this — do NOT introduce a pre-check like `if ! bao read ...; then`, because that races with concurrent bootstraps.

### Test tiers present in this project

- Unit: Go unit tests under `operators/keystone/internal/controller/*_test.go` — none needed for this policy-only change.
- Integration: envtest-based controller tests — none needed; no controller code changes.
- E2E: Chainsaw tests under `tests/e2e/` — yes, this is where the regression guard goes (REQ-005, Task 4.1-4.5).

No new unit or integration tests are appropriate because no Go code changes. The E2E tier is the right and only tier for this bug class.
